title: Aztec-inspired circular papercut
subtitle: Designing and cutting a radially symmetrical circular pattern
excerpt: A circular papercut with radial symmetry is built from a folded paper disc. The number of folds determines the degree of symmetry: four folds produce eight-fold symmetry; three folds produce six-fold. The Aztec calendar stone and similar pre-Columbian circular designs are a strong reference for bold geometric shapes that translate cleanly to cut paper.
sourceNotes: The Aztec calendar stone (Piedra del Sol, circa 15th century, public domain image via Wikimedia Commons) as a design reference for radially symmetrical geometric papercut patterns. The fold-cut technique for radial symmetry is part of the broader folk papercut tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: A design property in which the pattern repeats around a central point. In a folded circular papercut, the number of repeats equals twice the number of folds: four folds produce eight identical segments.
The wedge-shaped section of a folded paper disc, representing one repeat unit of the radial pattern. Cuts made within the folded segment are repeated in every other segment when the disc is opened.

An uncut strip of paper connecting two cut areas within a papercut design. Bridges maintain the structural integrity of the cut by preventing sections from becoming detached. Every element of the design must be connected to the rest by at least one bridge.

. Every cut made in the segment is repeated in all other segments when the disc opens. The critical design rule: every element must be connected to its neighbours by at least one

items.name: Coloured craft paper, square, 20 x 20 cm
items.notes: A square is needed to fold into a circle. 80 to 120 gsm cuts cleanly; heavier card is difficult to fold into multiple layers.

items.name: Scalpel, 10A blade
items.notes: Required for the geometric interior cuts. Replace the blade if it drags.

items.name: Cutting mat
items.notes: Self-healing mat protects the table and provides grip.

items.name: Pencil
items.notes: For drawing the segment design before cutting.

items.name: Light box (optional)
items.notes: Useful for tracing a reference design onto the folded segment.

content.text: Fold the square in half diagonally to make a triangle. Fold the triangle in half again (point to point) to make a smaller triangle. Fold once more in the same direction. The resulting wedge is one-eighth of the full circle: the tip of the wedge is the centre of the disc, and the curved outer edge (you will trim this) becomes the outer rim. Mark the outer curve with pencil and trim with scissors to produce a clean arc.

Draw the design on the outer face of the folded wedge. Work from the outer rim inward toward the centre tip. Use geometric shapes: triangles, stepped cuts, diamond voids, narrow channels. Plan bridges into the design so no element is fully cut away from the surrounding paper. Cut through all eight layers simultaneously with the scalpel and cutting mat, pressing firmly and completing each cut in one pass.

Unfold the disc slowly and carefully, easing the layers apart. Any cuts that go fully to a fold line will sever the connection between segments. If a segment detaches, it can be repaired with a small piece of matching paper and glue stick on the back. Press the opened disc under a heavy book for fifteen minutes to flatten the fold lines before mounting.
